core_behavior.note: Model A specifies pure RBAC, Model B specifies RBAC with ABAC extensions and a detailed (principal, action, resource) evaluation model, and Model C specifies a generic policy-based model defaulting to RBAC; the requirement did not specify which access control model to use.

inputs.note: Model B specifies a structured set of typed fields including principal_type, resource_id, resource_owner_id, and context object, while Models A and C specify higher-level inputs such as credentials and access request details without that granularity.

outputs.note: Model B returns only a binary PERMIT/DENY decision, Model A returns success/error responses for both authentication and authorization, and Model C returns an access decision plus detailed audit logs.

assumptions.note: Model A assumes hierarchical RBAC with a guest default role and coarse-grained permissions, Model B assumes authenticated principals and runtime enforcement only, and Model C assumes RBAC unless otherwise specified with an external authentication service; the access control model choice is an unspecified design assumption.

failure_modes.note: Model C specifies four distinct failure scenarios including policy retrieval failure and system downtime defaulting to deny, Model A covers authentication failure and generic errors, and Model B only addresses role inheritance cycles as a failure mode.

edge_cases.note: Model A addresses multiple/conflicting roles and null inputs, Model B addresses wildcard matching and explicit deny precedence, and Model C addresses expired credentials, concurrent requests, and non-existent resources.

security_constraints.note: Model A specifies password hashing algorithms and encryption, Model B specifies deny-by-default, contextual attributes including MFA and device trust, and cycle detection, while Model C specifies encryption, rate limiting, account lockout, and regulatory compliance.

performance_constraints.note: Model B has an empty performance_constraints array while Models A and C specify latency thresholds and scalability requirements.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in four of the eight fields, reflecting that the underspecified requirement left multiple design dimensions unconstrained.
